name: Scientific Model Scoring SMOTE and Image Augmentation
population_or_context: High-school students' computer-based electroscope scientific models, including drawn/model components and written explanations, scored with a 13-category analytic rubric.
sample_size: Each category dataset consisted of 1121 student model images with binary rubric labels; the article describes two model collections and a separate independent testing phase.
methods: CNN-based automated scoring models were trained and evaluated under no augmentation, general image augmentation, and SMOTE strategies. SMOTE generated feature-space samples for imbalanced rubric categories and was applied only to training data.
education_context: Physical science/electroscope modeling task asking students to construct and explain scientific models.
response_origin: Real student scientific models plus image augmentation and SMOTE-generated feature-space samples; not synthetic prose student answers.
rubric_label_space: Thirteen binary analytic rubric categories capturing components, relationships, explanations, and inaccuracies in student scientific models.
label_source: Human scoring by trained raters with discrepancies resolved through team discussion and Krippendorff's alpha of at least 0.8 before formal scoring.
generation_model: No text-generation model; CNN scorers used image data augmentation and imbalanced-learn SMOTE feature-space interpolation.
generation_method: General image transformations and SMOTE interpolation among minority-class feature representations for each rubric category.
evaluation_method: Tenfold cross-validation during training and independent testing, reporting accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 across rubric categories.
scorer_use: Training balancing and robustness analysis for automated scoring of multimodal constructed responses.
real_response_comparator: Yes. Independent testing used real student model responses, with SMOTE confined to the training set.
sparsity_or_imbalance_motivation: The article reports highly imbalanced rubric categories, including categories with positive cases below 10%, and notes that complex assessment tasks often lack high-performance responses.
synthetic_data_research_role: SMOTE and image augmentation supplement imbalanced training data to reduce majority-class bias and improve automated scoring of underrepresented response categories.
reported_metrics: For severe imbalance categories, average recall reportedly increased from 0.52 before SMOTE to 0.89 after SMOTE; Category 11 F1 increased from 0.47 to 0.86; Category 8 recall increased from 0.70 to 0.86 after SMOTE.
limitations: This is adjacent evidence for constructed-response augmentation because the synthetic samples are image/feature-space training examples, not generated textual student responses. The authors note SMOTE does not inherently address conceptual differences in synthetic samples.
